frameworks.name: bounded evidence accumulation
frameworks.one_line: noisy sensory evidence integrates to a decision bound over time

frameworks.name: discrete stepping (rapid commitment)
frameworks.one_line: single-trial activity jumps in discrete steps rather than ramping

frameworks.name: trial-averaged ramping
frameworks.one_line: smooth ramps in averaged firing may mask underlying spiking dynamics

core_question: Do single-trial spiking statistics in superior colliculus delay-period activity reflect accumulation or stepping, across different decision tasks?
key_assumptions: second-order spike-train statistics discriminate accumulation from stepping
trial-averaged ramping can be produced by multiple latent processes
spike-train simulations capture the candidate latent dynamics
manipulations: task type (motion direction discrimination vs orientation detection vs delayed saccade)
key_findings: motion discrimination delay activity is statistically consistent with accumulation
orientation detection delay activity superficially ramps but is statistically consistent with stepping
simple delayed-saccade activity matches neither accumulation nor stepping
simulations reproduce both accumulation and stepping signatures
interpretations: delay-period SC activity reflects task-dependent latent dynamics, not a single universal accumulation process
differences cannot be attributed to attention, reward, or motor planning since saccade controls also differ
